All Questions
Were You Asked This?
Design an Online Game Leaderboard

Design a real-time gaming leaderboard system that displays global top rankings, user's current rank with surrounding players, and rankings within their friends list, handling millions of score updates daily.

Asked at:

Meta

Google

PayPal

Try This Problem Yourself

Practice with guided hints and real-time feedback

Start Practice

An online game leaderboard is the ranking service a game backend pushes match results into. After every match it computes the player's new season rating, updates the rankings within a few seconds, and lets players see where they stand globally and against their friends. Think of the season ladders in Fortnite, Apex Legends, or a competitive coding platform. You're designing for 500M daily active players and 500M score updates a day, which averages near 6K writes per second but peaks around 50K after tournaments and daily resets, plus roughly 120K leaderboard reads per second at peak, all with sub-200ms p99 reads and rankings fresh within about 5 seconds.

The interesting parts aren't the raw QPS. They're surviving a tournament-sized write burst without dropping accepted results, computing an exact global rank without scanning a 500M-row ordered index, keeping ingestion idempotent so a duplicate or out-of-order result can't overwrite a newer score, and serving a friends leaderboard where one user has 15 friends and another has 8,000 without a fan-out strategy that blows up write throughput. An interviewer uses this question to see whether you pick the simplest design that hits the freshness and latency targets at this volume, and whether you reach for the right consistency tradeoff instead of insisting on a perfectly consistent global ranking after every write.

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

The system accepts authoritative match results, computes each player's new season rating, and serves the top-K players on the global leaderboard.

Users can see their current global rank and the K players immediately above and below them.

Users can see a leaderboard scoped to their friends, with their rank and the K friends above and below them.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How do you keep the match-result ingestion path durable and resilient when a tournament drives traffic far above the average write rate?

How do you compute an exact global rank and serve top-K at sub-200ms p99 when the leaderboard has hundreds of millions of players?

How do you make the ingestion pipeline idempotent so a duplicate or out-of-order match result can't overwrite a newer score?

How do you serve the friends leaderboard when one user has 15 friends and another has 8,000?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Scaling Writes

Ingestion is 500M results a day with multi-x bursts after tournaments, and two of the deep dives are straight out of this pattern: putting a durable log in front of processing, partitioning it by userId so consumers scale horizontally and a player's events stay ordered, using consumer lag plus backpressure to protect the system, and making the upsert idempotent with a version check so duplicates and out-of-order events can't corrupt a score.

Read More
Scaling Reads

The read path carries 120K reads per second at sub-200ms p99 for rank, neighbors, and top-K over 500M players. The article's playbook is exactly what the global-rank deep dive needs: move the ranking structure into memory, shard it (by score range here so rank is shard-local rank plus higher-shard counts), serve top-K from the top shards only, and cache the hottest results behind a short TTL.

Read More
Real-time Updates

Players expect their rank to move within about 5 seconds of finishing a match, so this is a streaming-ingestion system whose whole quality bar is freshness. The pattern frames the push-versus-pull refresh choices and, more importantly here, the consumer-lag-as-freshness-budget thinking that decides how aggressively you can batch or defer leaderboard updates.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Kafka

The durable log between match-result ingestion and leaderboard processing. The API appends a result and acks only after it's durable, so a tournament burst lands in the log instead of overwhelming the datastore. Partitioning by userId gives sequential per-player processing for the version-check idempotency story and lets you scale consumers per partition, and replay after an outage falls out for free.

Read More
Redis

The ranking layer. Sorted sets sharded by score range hold the global leaderboard with ZREVRANK for local rank, range queries for neighbors, and atomically-maintained per-shard counts; a short-TTL top-K cache absorbs read spikes; pipelined ZSCORE lookups serve fan-out-on-read friends leaderboards with a precomputed sorted set for celebrity-sized friend lists; and Lua scripts make the dedup-check, score-write, and version-bump a single atomic step.

Read More
PostgreSQL

The system of record for scores and friendships and the durable source for rebuilding the Redis ranking layer after an incident. The Scores table keyed by (userId, seasonId) with a version and timestamp is what the consumer reads before deciding whether an event is stale, and the Friends table backs the friend-list lookups that the friends leaderboard fans out over.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

YouTube Top K

Same core problem: maintain a frequently-changing ranked list under heavy writes and answer rank and top-K queries fast. The techniques transfer directly, sharding the ranking structure, computing a global position from a shard-local position plus prefix counts, serving the head from the top shards, and trading exactness for throughput on the long tail.

Read
Practice
Ad Click Aggregator

The ingestion side is the same shape: a firehose of events through a durable log, partitioned and coalesced per entity before they're served to queries, processed idempotently, replayable after a failure, with an explicit eventual-consistency tradeoff. The match-result pipeline and the click-event pipeline are the same design with different payloads.

Read
Practice

Online Chess

Chess has the same leaderboard requirement at its core: an authoritative event at game completion produces a new rating, which feeds a global top-N plus per-user neighbor reads on a known cadence rather than continuously, so the same cache-friendly, batch-then-publish ranking design applies on both sides.

Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

One monolithic sorted set or table for the whole global leaderboard

A single ranking structure is a hot partition and a single point of failure, and it can't carry 500M players, 50K writes per second, and sub-200ms rank queries at once. The unstated requirement is to shard the ranking layer. A strong candidate reaches for score-range shards with per-shard counts early so rank is a local rank plus higher-shard counts; a weak one tries to make one big sorted set work.

Synchronous datastore writes in the ingestion request path

Writing each match result straight to the leaderboard datastore in the request path means there's no buffer when rating computation or the datastore gets slow, so a tournament spike turns into request failures and dropped results. Push the candidate on what happens to accepted results when the write path stalls; the answer needs a durable log that makes results durable before the ack and processes them asynchronously.

Synchronous query-and-sort of every friend from the primary store per request

That's read amplification proportional to friend-list size, so tail latency blows up under load and it's hopeless for a celebrity-sized list. A strong candidate pipelines cached score lookups, sorts a couple hundred entries in memory, and does the fan-out-on-write math before reaching for it, landing on a hybrid; a weak one never notices that one strategy can't cover both a 15-friend user and an 8,000-friend user.

Two-step check-then-write deduplication on an at-least-once queue

Checking a processed-event store and then marking it processed in a separate step leaves a window where a crash double-applies a result or drops it, and it does nothing about a stale event arriving after a newer one. A strong candidate rejects events with an older version, does the dedup check, score write, and version bump atomically (a Lua script), and commits the queue offset only after the write succeeds; if the candidate hand-waves idempotency, push on the crash and reordering cases.

Mid October, 2025

Meta

A Gaming Leaderboard is a ranked list of players, typically sorted by a specific metric such as score, points or level.
In a real-time leaderboard, updates happen almost instantly:

A player’s score changes (e.g., after scoring a point or defeating an opponent).

The system updates that player’s rank immediately.

Other players can see the updated position without waiting or refreshing.

This real-time aspect makes the user experience more dynamic and engaging.

However, it also introduces significant technical challenges, such as:

Efficiently retrieving Top-N players (e.g., Top 10 or Top 100).

Allowing players to quickly find their own rank without scanning the entire leaderboard.

In this article, we will explore how to design a scalable, low-latency, and real-time leaderboard that can support above queries and enhance the user experience.

amitpandit09

In general, how we tackle the conflict in case of distributed REDIS cluster ?

Shard by UserId OR
Shard by Scores OR
Shard by game (this is unlikely bcz interviewer will ask us to scale...nd push to discuss on (1) and (2))

(1) helps in achieving user (view user scores, ranking etc) specific queries in minimal delay but slows down group queries i.e friends , view k scores above-below user + top k global list of users
(2) helps in achieving top k global list of users lightning fast but getting a specific user's information becomes very difficult because you don't know which shard the user is currently living on.

How we should determine which to use ? Shall we use both together and create a hybrid approach ?

1

Reply
D
DiplomaticJadePeacock784
• 5个月前

Scores won't be realistic to maintain, basically you may need to reshard on every score update. Also, I don't think we need it.
I think you can maintain 2 redis instances, 1 sharded by userId and 1 by gameId.
Then you use userId for anything global:

global rank & score
getting friends and friends scores (not sure whether this was for game or not, if it is then just the friends themselves).
k scores above and below in global leaderboard

gameId
for top N leaderboards per time window (only holds aggregated data / tumbling window)

1

TerritorialSalmonHookworm256
Premium
• 5个月前

How to do get K scores above and below if you shard by userId? I imagine you have to read all shards and merge the result. If we shard by score ranges, users need to move to another shard only when their scores across the range boundary, not every score update, and it's very predictable, usually are the neighbors.

1

M
macre.john
Premium
• 23天前

If we wanted to support some type of approximate ranking, modelling this as a stream processing problem with sparse exponential histograms could be an interesting approach that means we can stay on a single Redis node + replicas. Flink-like -> local histograms tracking tumbling window deltas per bucket -> merge to primary histogram (people in each score range, basically) & cache -> replicate. Makes it a lot easier to check e.g. "how much did my rank change in the past month" if we can store some smaller snapshots into cold storage. A single Redis node is also probably viable up to decent scale with Redis sentinel (w/ connection pooling/pipelining) but may not be the vector the interviewer is interested in.

Evan King

Admin
• 8个月前

mentioned above in the first deep dive!

Explain sharding and rank computation. A common approach is to shard a Redis ZSET (or similar) by score ranges or hash of userId and maintain per-shard sorted sets plus shard-level cardinalities. Compute exact rank by combining local rank (ZRANK/ZCOUNT within the shard) with prefix sums of counts from higher-score shards maintained in a small, frequently refreshed index. Discuss write coalescing (e.g., Kafka with compaction keyed by userId), monotonic scores, and update rate-limiting to avoid thrashing.

Also check out the peer submissions above where they take a stab

amitpandit09
Premium
• 5个月前

In general, how we tackle the conflict in case of distributed REDIS cluster ?

Shard by UserId OR
Shard by Scores OR
Shard by game (this is unlikely bcz interviewer will ask us to scale...nd push to discuss on (1) and (2))

(1) helps in achieving user (view user scores, ranking etc) specific queries in minimal delay but slows down group queries i.e friends , view k scores above-below user + top k global list of users
(2) helps in achieving top k global list of users lightning fast but getting a specific user's information becomes very difficult because you don't know which shard the user is currently living on.

How we should determine which to use ? Shall we use both together and create a hybrid approach ?

D
DiplomaticJadePeacock784
• 5个月前

Scores won't be realistic to maintain, basically you may need to reshard on every score update. Also, I don't think we need it.
I think you can maintain 2 redis instances, 1 sharded by userId and 1 by gameId.
Then you use userId for anything global:

global rank & score
getting friends and friends scores (not sure whether this was for game or not, if it is then just the friends themselves).
k scores above and below in global leaderboard

gameId
for top N leaderboards per time window (only holds aggregated data / tumbling window)

How to do get K scores above and below if you shard by userId? I imagine you have to read all shards and merge the result. If we shard by score ranges, users need to move to another shard only when their scores across the range boundary, not every score update, and it's very predictable, usually are the neighbors.

M
macre.john
Premium
• 23天前

If we wanted to support some type of approximate ranking, modelling this as a stream processing problem with sparse exponential histograms could be an interesting approach that means we can stay on a single Redis node + replicas. Flink-like -> local histograms tracking tumbling window deltas per bucket -> merge to primary histogram (people in each score range, basically) & cache -> replicate. Makes it a lot easier to check e.g. "how much did my rank change in the past month" if we can store some smaller snapshots into cold storage. A single Redis node is also probably viable up to decent scale with Redis sentinel (w/ connection pooling/pipelining) but may not be the vector the interviewer is interested in.

Reply

Evan King

Admin
• 8个月前

mentioned above in the first deep dive!

Explain sharding and rank computation. A common approach is to shard a Redis ZSET (or similar) by score ranges or hash of userId and maintain per-shard sorted sets plus shard-level cardinalities. Compute exact rank by combining local rank (ZRANK/ZCOUNT within the shard) with prefix sums of counts from higher-score shards maintained in a small, frequently refreshed index. Discuss write coalescing (e.g., Kafka with compaction keyed by userId), monotonic scores, and update rate-limiting to avoid thrashing.

Also check out the peer submissions above where they take a stab

meta.title: Design an Online Game Leaderboard
meta.description: Design a real-time gaming leaderboard system that displays global top rankings, user's current rank with surrounding players, and rankings within their friends list, handling millions of score updates daily.
